important_rules.text: ALLIES: an ally is anyone you consider to be your ally in the current scene; if an effect specifically targets allies (not simply creatures or characters), you cannot use it on yourself. ALWAYS ROUND DOWN: when you halve or divide numbers, always round down to a minimum of 0. ORDER OF OPERATIONS: if you must perform multiple operations on the same number, do so in this order: additions, then subtractions, then multiplications, then divisions. SPECIFIC BEATS GENERAL: if a specific element of the game contradicts a general rule, the specific element takes precedence. TIMING ISSUES: if choices/effects are controlled by the same person, that person chooses the order; if by different Player Characters, those Players agree (randomly determine if they cannot); if some are controlled by PCs and others by NPCs, Player Characters always go first.

character.text: Traits: each character has three Traits — Identity (a short sentence summing up the concept, e.g. 'Sky Pirate'), Theme (a strong ideal or sentiment such as Anger, Justice, or Ambition), and Origin (where the character comes from, their homeland). Traits can be invoked to improve die rolls. Character Level: an abstract indication of power; characters generally begin at level 5 and can climb to level 50. On average you gain enough Experience Points to reach a new level at the end of every other session. Classes and Skills: each level is put into one of fifteen Classes; each Class grants Skills and abilities. When the same Skill is taken multiple times its Skill Level (SL) increases. Defense represents the ability to avoid being hit (typically based on Dexterity, via dodging or shield/armor); Magic Defense represents the ability to anticipate and resist offensive spells (mostly based on Insight). Initiative Modifier indicates the ability to act quickly in a conflict scene. Equipment: a character can equip weapons, shields, armor, and accessories; anything not equipped is stored in the backpack; consumables are represented by Inventory Points. Zenit is the common currency used in the worlds of Fabula Ultima.

attributes.text: A character's Attributes represent training and aptitudes in four fields. Dexterity (DEX) measures precision, coordination, finesse and reflexes; used to move cautiously, defend from attacks, craft objects, and fight with light weapons, bows, and firearms. Insight (INS) represents observation, understanding, and reasoning; important when investigating, casting spells, and defending from magic. Might (MIG) measures strength and fortitude; most heavy weapons rely on it, and it ties to Hit Points. Willpower (WLP) represents determination, charisma, and discipline; used for diplomacy and persuasion, and it ties to Mind Points. Each Attribute is a die size ranging from d6 up to d12. Some effects temporarily alter the current Attribute die size, while the default size is the base Attribute die size.

check.text: A Check is a die roll based on a character's core Attributes: Dexterity, Insight, Might, and Willpower. Characters perform Checks when required by the rules or when asked by the Game Master; only the GM can ask for a Check, Players never may. Effects that do not explicitly call for a Check always succeed automatically. A Check is presented as a formula and always requires you to roll exactly two dice, which are added together. Some Checks also add or subtract a modifier (e.g. a MIG + MIG +5 Check rolls the Might die twice and then adds 5). Terminology — Modifier: any value added to (bonus) or subtracted from (penalty) a Check. Result: the final number after applying modifiers. Difficulty Level (DL): if the Result is greater than or equal to this number, the action succeeds. High Roll (HR): the higher of the two dice rolled, used to calculate various effects (typically weapon and spell damage).

difficulty_levels.who: Anyone with a bit of training or natural talent.
A competent person, or a very talented one.
Someone who is among the very best in that field.

difficulty_note.text: If you are the Game Master and find yourself stumped when choosing an appropriate Difficulty Level, use 10: it is neither too low nor too high, and it's easy to remember.

critical.text: When both dice rolled during a Check show the same number, and that number is 6 or higher, the Result is a critical success (a double 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, or 12). A critical success is automatically successful and you get to apply the effects of an opportunity.

fumble.text: When both dice rolled during a Check show a 1, the Result is a fumble: no matter the modifiers, a fumbled Check is always a failure. When you roll a fumble, whoever controls your opposition in this scene gets an opportunity. When a Player Character rolls a fumble, they immediately earn 1 Fabula Point.

high_roll.text: High Roll (HR) indicates the higher of the two dice rolled in a Check and is used to calculate various effects (typically weapon and spell damage). For instance, if you roll d8 + d10 and the dice show a 6 and a 9, the HR is 9. During Accuracy Checks the HR determines how much damage you deal; during Magic Checks the HR sometimes determines the effectiveness of the spell cast.

opportunities.name: Advantage
opportunities.text: The next Check performed by you or an ally will receive a +4 bonus.

opportunities.name: Affliction
opportunities.text: A creature suffers dazed, shaken, slow or weak.

opportunities.name: Bonding
opportunities.text: You create a Bond towards someone or something or add an emotion to one of your existing Bonds.

opportunities.name: Faux Pas
opportunities.text: Choose a creature present on the scene: they make a compromising statement chosen by the person who controls them.

opportunities.name: Favor
opportunities.text: Your actions earn you someone's support or admiration.

opportunities.name: Information
opportunities.text: You spot a useful clue or detail. The Game Master may tell you what it is, or ask you to introduce that detail yourself.

opportunities.name: Lost Item
opportunities.text: An item is destroyed, lost, stolen, or left behind.

opportunities.name: Progress
opportunities.text: You may fill or erase up to two sections on a Clock.

opportunities.name: Plot Twist!
opportunities.text: Someone or something of your choice suddenly appears on the scene.

opportunities.name: Scan
opportunities.text: You discover one Vulnerability or one Trait of a creature you can see.

opportunities.name: Unmask
opportunities.text: You learn the goals and motivations of a creature of your choice.

opportunities_note.text: Opportunities are unexpected twists in the story — sometimes good, sometimes bad. When you spend an opportunity, you may pick an option from the list or come up with a different twist that fits the current scene. The Game Master has final say on whether an opportunity is appropriate to the current situation.

check_variants.success_at_a_cost: Optional rule: when a character fails a Check, anyone may propose a success at a cost; the GM may adjust the cost and the Player who performed the Check may accept or refuse. The cost should put the character in a rough spot or take something important away. You cannot succeed at a cost if you fumbled the Check. When you succeed at a cost, the Check's High Roll and Result remain the same.
check_variants.invoke_to_fail: Optional rule: once per scene before making a Check, a Player may describe how the character's Bonds or Traits prevent them from achieving success — this is done instead of performing the Check. The Check is then treated as an automatic failure with a Result and High Roll equal to 0, and the character gains 1 Fabula Point. You cannot invoke to fail on Support Checks, and you cannot succeed at a cost on a Check you already invoked to fail.
check_variants.accuracy: An Accuracy Check works like a normal Attribute Check but is performed when striking targets ready to defend themselves. Each weapon lists a specific Accuracy formula (e.g. a Steel Dagger requires DEX + INS +1). The Difficulty Level is generally equal to the target's Defense score. The High Roll is used to determine how much damage you deal.
check_variants.magic: A Magic Check works like a normal Attribute Check but is used when casting an offensive spell or performing a Ritual; the Attributes depend on the kind of magic used. The High Roll will sometimes be used to determine the effectiveness of the spell cast.
check_variants.opposed: Each character taking part performs the same Attribute Check with no Difficulty Level: whoever gets the highest Result wins. On a draw, the tied characters repeat the Check until one wins. Fumbles are the lowest possible Result and critical successes are the highest possible Result; they generate opportunities as normal, but if two or more characters roll a fumble or a critical success, these count as a draw and the Checks are repeated.

open.text: An Open Check has no Difficulty Level; instead the Result tells us how well the character performed. Compare the Result with the table below. Often used when researching information, examining an area, or recalling important lore.

table.equates_to: What could be accomplished by a person with a bit of training or talent.
What could be achieved by someone competent or very talented.
The work of an expert or true prodigy.
A result worthy of being remembered in history.

check_variants.situational_modifiers: If a character performs a Check while in a strongly advantageous or disadvantageous position, the Game Master may impose a +2 bonus or -2 penalty to the Result. Most of the time adjusting the Difficulty Level is enough, but situational modifiers are useful when Difficulty is established by the rules (such as an Accuracy Check) or during Opposed Checks.

group.text: Group Checks let characters cooperate. (1) The characters nominate a leader who performs the final Check; everyone else is a supporting character. (2) Each supporting character performs a Support Check identical to the leader's but with a fixed Difficulty Level of 10. Fumbles and critical successes rolled during Support Checks generate no opportunities but still count as automatic failures and successes. (3) Each supporting character that succeeds grants a +1 bonus to the leader's Check. (4) If any successful supporting characters have a Bond towards the leader, the single highest strength among those Bonds is also added to the leader's Check. (5) The leader performs the final Check, which works as normal and may generate opportunities on a fumble or critical success; the outcome affects everyone who took part.
group.leader_bond_bonus: highest single Bond strength among successful supporters who have a Bond towards the leader

check_variants.shared_attribute_choice: Optional rule: Players can decide one of the Attributes involved in each Check they perform, while the Game Master decides the other (same or different). Does not apply to Checks whose Attributes are already established by the rules, such as Accuracy Checks.

frequently_used.situation: Moving silently, hiding and acting unnoticed.
Avoiding a trap or finding a way to flee a collapsing building.
Anticipating someone's movements and catching them by surprise.
Completing a work of craftsmanship or repairing something.
Moving gracefully to earn someone's attention.
Examining or investigating someone or something.
Getting information from someone during a conversation.
Persuading someone through authority or diplomacy.
Hard work, such as lifting a portcullis or pushing a statue.

invoking.text: Invoking a Trait to reroll: after performing a Check, a Player Character may spend 1 Fabula Point and invoke one of their Traits (Identity, Origin, Theme) to immediately pick up one or both dice and roll them again, replacing the old roll with the new one. This may be done any number of times as part of the same invocation, but each new reroll costs another Fabula Point. You cannot invoke a Trait if you fumbled the Check. Invoking a Bond: after performing a Check, a Player Character may spend 1 Fabula Point and invoke one of their Bonds to add the strength of that Bond to the Result. This can only be done once per Check. Players should describe their actions coherently with the invoked Trait or Bond; do not invoke when it feels forced.

clocks.text: Clocks (also timers, counters, trackers, or countdowns) track an evolving situation, an approaching danger, or the characters' progress. A Clock is a circle split into a number of sections, normally four to twelve depending on complexity. Clocks are generally created and managed by the Game Master but should be visible to everyone. Advancing a Clock: in general Clocks advance through Checks. Fill one section for a successful Check; fill an additional section if the Result surpassed the Difficulty Level (or the opponent's Result in an Opposed Check) by 3 or more, or two additional sections if by 6 or more; if the Check was a critical success, the corresponding opportunity may be spent to fill two additional sections. Threat Clocks fill as characters fail Checks: fill one section for a failed Check; an additional section if the Result is lower than the DL by 3 or more, or two additional sections if by 6 or more; if the Check was a fumble, the opportunity may be spent to fill two additional sections. Turning back a Clock uses the same method as filling it; when a Clock reaches 0 the threat may be removed. If the GM feels an event (not tied to a Check) should fill or erase sections, they may fill or erase one section, or two for a major event. Time-tracker Clocks normally can't be directly influenced through Checks.

bonds.text: Each Bond may feature up to three emotions from six, combined into three pairings, and the same Bond may only be connected to one emotion from each pairing: Admiration or Inferiority; Loyalty or Mistrust; Affection or Hatred. Admiration — you believe you have much to learn from this person and deeply respect them. Inferiority — you envy this person or feel powerless against them. Loyalty — this person has won your trust or you believe in their ideals. Mistrust — you don't believe their words and doubt their intentions. Affection — you have tender feelings for this person. Hatred — you can scarcely control yourself in their presence and would do anything to see them defeated. For each emotion in a Bond, the strength of that Bond is increased by one (up to a strength of 3 if you have an emotion in each pairing). A character may have up to six Bonds at the same time. Bonds are generally created or changed during resting scenes, through opportunities, or due to specific Skills; when you 'create a Bond' you tie a single emotion to it, so all Bonds start at strength 1. You can have Bonds towards characters, nations, kingdoms, organizations, and religions, but not towards yourself. If you already have six Bonds and want a new one, you must first erase an existing Bond. Bonds are stronger than death: even if a character dies or leaves the story, any Bonds others had with them remain until erased.

gain.trigger: A Player Character has no Fabula Points at the start of a session.
A Player Character rolls a fumble on a Check.

A Villain makes an entrance during a scene (even a Game Master scene where the PCs are not present); each Player Character receives this. Maximum 3 per PC per scene from Villains; only 1 at the beginning of back-to-back scenes with the same Villain.
A Player Character is reduced to 0 Hit Points and decides to Surrender.
A Player Character invokes one of their Bonds or Traits to automatically fail a Check (optional invoke-to-fail rule).

spend.text: Alter an existing element or add a new element to the current situation.
Add Bond strength to a Check.
Reroll one or both dice during a Check.
Use a Skill that requires Fabula Points.

fabula_points.text: Each newly created character automatically receives 3 Fabula Points; any others must be gained during play. There is no upper limit to a character's Fabula Points, but spending them is one of the main ways to gain Experience Points, so saving them up slows the whole group's growth. Altering the Story: spend 1 Fabula Point to make a statement about the story — you shape part of the world to fill in grey areas, tweak a detail in your favor, or introduce a plot twist. You need the GM's permission to change or add details to something already established, and permission from the corresponding Player if it directly affects another Player Character. You cannot contradict a previous statement, and you cannot cause mechanical changes such as gaining/altering a Skill, switching a creature's Vulnerabilities, or inflicting a status effect. A new NPC introduced this way remains under the GM's control.

hp_mp.text: Hit Points (HP) indicate a character's vital energy, fighting spirit, and body health — their ability to withstand physical pain and fatigue; losing HP does not directly translate into wounds. Current HP can never go below 0 nor above maximum HP. Maximum HP caps current HP (healing above it is wasted). Crisis is equal to half of maximum HP, rounded down; if current HP is at or below this number the character is 'in Crisis'. If HP reaches 0, the character's fighting spirit has broken and they face consequences based on their role (Player Character, Villain, or normal NPC). Mind Points (MP) measure a character's inner force, magical potential, and focus, often spent to fuel magic or heroic feats. Current MP can never go below 0 nor above maximum MP. Spells require spending MP; you can only do so if you have enough for the full cost. If MP reaches 0 the character is mentally exhausted but suffers no other consequence aside from being unable to use MP-consuming abilities. Maximum HP equals total character level + five times base Might die size; maximum MP equals total character level + five times base Willpower die size; Defense equals current Dexterity die size; Magic Defense equals current Insight die size.

zero_hp.crisis: Crisis is equal to half of a character's maximum HP, rounded down; if your current HP is at or below this number you are considered 'in Crisis'. For instance, a character with 65 maximum HP will be in Crisis if they have 32 Hit Points or less.
zero_hp.surrender: Characters who surrender fall unconscious and become unable to act for the remainder of the scene, even if their Hit Points are restored above 0. The Game Master cannot kill a character who surrenders but may impose a narrative consequence chosen from the list below or a consequence that makes sense within the scene. If multiple characters surrender — or the entire group is wiped out — the Game Master may impose separate consequences or a single major consequence, and may bring the consequences of your surrender into play later on. Permanent or crippling injuries should be avoided. When a Player Character chooses to Surrender, they immediately gain 2 Fabula Points. At the start of the next scene in which they appear, the Player Character regains consciousness and recovers an amount of Hit Points equal to their Crisis score. Sample consequences of surrendering — Despair: you must change your Theme to one of the following: Anger, Doubt, Guilt, or Vengeance (your choice). Loss: the enemy gets to make a decisive move, or the heroes lose the faith and approval of an important person or group; or something incredibly precious, such as a magical artifact, a loved person, or an ancient and important heirloom, is taken from you. Resentment: you are forced to erase one of your Bonds and replace it with a Bond towards a character chosen by the Game Master — this new Bond must be of hatred, inferiority, or mistrust (your choice). Separated: you are no longer with your allies; you might be captured, dragged away, lost, or stranded in some unknown location.
zero_hp.sacrifice: When reduced to 0 Hit Points, a Player Character may give their life in order to accomplish a seemingly impossible deed, such as putting an end to a centuries-old curse, temporarily taking away a demigod's powers, or single-handedly holding off a small army to make sure their allies can flee. You may only sacrifice yourself if at least two of the following are true: a Villain is present on the scene; your sacrifice would benefit a character you have a Bond towards; you believe your sacrifice would make the world a better place. The details of the sacrifice are negotiated between you and the Game Master — you should narrate your heroic demise together. There is no fixed limit on what a sacrifice may accomplish; you can and should go overboard with this, especially as you will permanently lose your character. Player Characters who sacrifice themselves have accepted their death and normally cannot be brought back to life, because they peacefully became one with the stream of souls.
zero_hp.text: When a character's Hit Points fall to 0, defeated characters have different options depending on their role. Non-Player Characters: an NPC reduced to 0 HP loses all will to fight, and whoever defeated them gets to determine their fate (captured, forced to flee, knocked unconscious, or slain); some creatures such as constructs, elementals, and undead may simply be destroyed. Villains: when reduced to 0 HP a Villain must choose to escape (spend 1 Ultima Point to safely disappear; if no Ultima Points remain they may escalate) or surrender (treated as any other NPC). Player Characters: when reduced to 0 HP a Player Character must either Sacrifice themselves or Surrender. Resurrection: the mechanics do not give the option of bringing characters back to life, both because a Player Character's death should be meaningful and because the mystery of the afterlife should be explored together as part of the story.

resting.text: Resting in the wilderness requires access to one of: a magic tent (created by spending 4 Inventory Points; one tent is enough for the entire group); hospitality from someone friendly; or a safe location with no risk of attack (a consecrated temple, secret room, armored transport, or magic-circle-protected area). Resting inside settlements requires access to one of: a room inside an inn (which costs money); or hospitality from someone friendly. The effects of resting: a Player Character recovers all Hit Points and Mind Points, and recovers from all status effects. Generally resting requires four to six hours of inactivity. Bonds and resting scenes: at the end of a resting scene each PC may do one of the following — create a new Bond towards someone or something; add a new emotion to an existing Bond; or erase an emotion from an existing Bond and replace it with a different one. These rules only apply to Player Characters; the GM controls NPC recovery, and NPCs are assumed to appear at full HP and MP with no status effects. Inactivity should never be without consequence — the GM may use Clocks to foreshadow incoming threats, filling a section at the end of each rest.

inventory_points.text: Player Characters have an abstract reserve of useful gear and consumable items, represented by Inventory Points (IP). A character can normally carry a maximum of 6 Inventory Points, though some Classes and special Skills increase this limit. Spending IP: to produce a consumable item, spend the appropriate amount of IP (all from the same character); once created the item must be used immediately and is destroyed. Default inventory items — Elixir (3 IP): one creature recovers 50 Mind Points; Remedy (3 IP): one creature recovers 50 Hit Points; Tonic (2 IP): one creature recovers from a single status effect; Elemental Shard (2 IP): one creature suffers 10 damage of a type of your choice (air, bolt, earth, fire, or ice); Magic Tent (4 IP): allows the entire group to rest in the wilderness. Recharging IP costs 10 zenit for every Inventory Point in a town, village, or similar location; current IP can never exceed maximum IP. Characters are not allowed to redistribute Inventory Points among themselves. Characters are assumed to carry basic adventuring gear appropriate to their Identity; for uncommon items that don't fit their Identity, the GM may allow spending IP (generally 2 to 4). Items pulled from the backpack this way are not immediately destroyed but should not be sold for more than five times their IP cost, and gear never grants a mechanical benefit.

travel.text: The basic unit of travel is the travel day: the average distance covered during a single day on the map on foot; its length is established during World Creation. For each travel day: (1) the Game Master determines the threat level of the areas traversed, applying only the highest if more than one is present; (2) perform a travel roll by rolling the die appropriate to the threat level — on a 6 or higher the group encounters a danger, on a 1 the group makes an unexpected discovery; (3) unless stopped by a danger, the characters proceed to the next travel day or reach their destination. Travel days are always rounded up to the next whole number. Threat levels and travel rolls: Minimal (within villages, cities and guarded areas) = d6; Low (prairies, patrolled roads, countrysides) = d8; Medium (forests, hills, rivers) = d10; High (vast forests, mountains, open sea, swamps) = d12; Very High (deserts, frozen wastes, jungles, volcanoes) = d20. Dangers can be overcome through a series of Checks or a conflict scene, and may deal damage (using the improvised/hazard damage table by level) or inflict status effects. Discoveries are always a good thing, even if it's a dangerous location — the reward should more than make up for it. Transports increase the distance covered per travel day.

economy.text: The common currency of all Fabula Ultima worlds is zenit, a gold coin. On average 5 z pays for a decent meal, 10 z rents a room for the night in town, and 100 z can fetch a bronze shield. Equipment slots: one for armor; a main hand (a one-handed or two-handed weapon; a two-handed weapon also takes the off-hand slot); an off-hand (a one-handed weapon or a shield); and one for an accessory. Unequipped items go in the backpack (no set limit, GM has final say); an item in the backpack grants none of its properties unless equipped, and during a conflict you must spend an action searching for a backpack item. Getting items: basic weapons, armor, and shields are available in most settlements, while rare items should only be found in unique stores, dangerous ruins, or offered as rewards. Rewards can be handled Classic (GM hands out rewards), Survey (GM asks what items players want, keeping a list to pick from), or Wishlist (each Player writes a list of desired items and picks from it when treasure is found). Selling items: an item sells for roughly half its original price, modified by quality and condition; the GM has final say. Artifacts are incredibly powerful magic items that should never be purchased or sold. Town/travel services (guideline prices) — Recharge Inventory: 10 z (recharges one IP); Full Rest: 5 z village / 10 z town / 20 z city (one person, one night); Land Transportation: 10 z / Water: 20 z / Air: 40 z (one person, one day). Transports (cost / passengers / distance multiplier) — Mounts: Terrestrial 200 z (one or two, x2), Aquatic 500 z (~six, x2), Submarine 1000 z (~six, x2), Flying 2000 z (~six, x3); Vehicles: Terrestrial 600 z (~six, x2), Aquatic 2000 z (~ten, x2), Submarine 4000 z (~ten, x2), Flying 8000 z (~twenty, x3).

projects.text: Characters with the Tinkerer Class may start Projects to work on custom inventions. (1) Describe the invention's effects, operation, energy, and benefits; the GM has final say on feasibility. (2) The GM may split a complex invention into two or more separate inventions, each with its own development. (3) Using the area, potency, and uses tables the GM determines the total cost in materials, paid immediately; for medium or higher potency the GM also describes a necessary special ingredient or material that cannot simply be purchased. (4) You may negotiate a terrible flaw with the GM, reducing the total cost by 25%. (5) To complete the Project you must reach progress equal to one for every 100 zenit of material costs (minimum one progress). At the end of each day: +1 progress for every PC who worked on it, +1 extra progress for every PC with one or more levels in the Tinkerer Class who worked on it. (6) Once the required progress is reached, the invention is created; generating more progress in a day than needed can finish it within a few hours. Potency base costs: Minor 100 zenit, Medium 200 zenit, Major 400 zenit, Extreme 800 zenit. Area multipliers: Individual x1, Small x2, Large x3, Huge x4. Uses multipliers: Consumable x1, Permanent x5. Damage/protection by potency: Minor (no damage / one Resistance), Medium (minor damage / two Resistances), Major (major damage / one Immunity or two Resistances), Extreme (massive damage / two Immunities or three Resistances); avoid granting Immunity to physical damage; damage/healing amounts use the level table (5+: 10/30/40, 20+: 20/40/60, 40+: 30/50/80). Precious materials gathered during adventures may pay for a Project's costs (but a required special ingredient cannot also be used to pay). Hiring helpers: each helper asks for pay equal to half the Project's total cost and generates 1 additional progress at the end of each day. Projects are for innovative, unique solutions, not for crafting normal equipment; each copy of an invention is a separate Project.

experience.text: A character's power is measured by their level: starting characters enter at level 5, and the upper limit is level 50. At the end of each game session a character gains: 5 XP automatically; plus XP equal to the amount of Ultima Points spent by Villains during the session; plus XP equal to the amount of Fabula Points spent by the group during the session, divided by the number of Player Characters that took part. When the game refers to 'a session' it means about four hours of play. After gaining XP, if you have 10 or more Experience Points you must spend ten of them to increase your character level by one (keeping any excess), but you may only gain one level per session this way. For each new level: you may change your Identity and/or Theme; increase your maximum Hit Points and Mind Points by one point each (this does not affect current HP/MP); if you just reached level 20 or 40, choose one Attribute and increase its base die size by one step, up to a maximum of d12 (the only way to ever increase Attributes); and increase the level of one of your Classes by one, or gain your first level in a new Class. Limitations: you can never have more than ten levels in a Class (the tenth level masters it, granting a Heroic Skill, after which you can no longer invest in it); and you can never have more than three non-mastered Classes. Maximum HP equals total character level + five times base Might die size (Crisis is half that, rounded down); maximum MP equals total character level + five times base Willpower die size; Defense equals current Dexterity die size; Magic Defense equals current Insight die size.
